La1' offices af
OLSON, CONNELLY HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH
rPPC
May 15, 1990
Scott Hallabrin
Actingr General counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 ttJtt street, Suite 800
Sacramento, california 
958L4
RE: REQUEST FOR IIITORI(AL ASSIATANCE
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
Thj.s letter is to request infornal assistance r^/ith
respect to the following question.
OUESTION
A public agency olrns land which it desires to have
developed in a manner which furthers its purposes. The
public agency wishes to provide a l-ong-term lease for
the land to any developer who will construct
improvements on the property and operate the
improvernents in the manner directed by the l-and-owner.
In order to attract applicants, the pubtic entity
offers the l-and at a nominal l-ease rent. fn return,
applicants must agree to rent the improvenents onfy to
qualified renters and at controlled (be1ow-market)
rents. In addition, applicants must construct all
improvements at the applicantts cost and to the
Landlordrs specifications. 
Furthemore, at the end of
LANCE H. olsoN 
the tong-tern tenancy, the j-mprovements become the
BRUCE J. HAGEL property of the public agency land-owner. No money
LERoy Y FoNG 
flows frorn the public agency to the eventuall-y selected
ROBERT E. LEIDTGH developer-tenant. The only incorne received by the
GEoRGE M. WATERS devel-oper-te:iarrt j-s frcn -Lhe q.dalified renters who rent
the improvenents.
Under the foregoing circumstances, am I correct in
oF CouNsEL rny analysis that the land-owner public entity is not a
LroyD G. coNNELLy. Menber rtsource of incometr under the Po1itical Reforn Act to
calilornia state Lesistature the developer-tenant. In the instant situation, the
applicant, if successful would be providing full
consideration for the lolrered leasehold cost of the
land by constructing the irnprovements and renting them
to only qualified renters at controlled rents.
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramentq California 95814
TELEPHoNE: (91,6) 442-2952
FAx, (91,6) 442-1280
llar 16 Z tr pr{'g


Scott HalLabrin
May 15, 1990
Page 2
DIACUSSION
To my knowledge, generally the commission has not concluded
that in such a situation the person being paid is a source of
incorne to the person doing the paying. 
Thus, even though an
employee provides consideration for his or her pay, he or she is
not viewed as a source of income to the ernployer. Likewise,
tenants are sources of income to landlords, but not vice versa.
Sirnilarly, a client is a source of j.ncorne to a lawyer or
accountant, but the lawyer or accountant is not a source of
income to the client merely by providing consideration in return
f or the paFrents by the cL ient . 
( see Dc!'n }-dvice Letter , I'lo . A--
83-086, at page 3.) 
Ho$/ever, in at least one instance, the
advice was different where a school district 
employee was
receiving subsidized rent frorn her empl-oyer. (see Wi]son Advice
Letter, No. A-81--036. )
consequently, I ask for your informal assj-stance in
resotving this question. your staffrs timely consj-deration of
this matter will be greatl,y appreciated.
very truly yours,
OLSON,
tY't,/ o. r-
I ROBERT
REL/k1
& LEIDIGH
CONNELLY ,
